<!--
Curated reader manuscript draft.
chapter_id: failure-modes-of-ungoverned-intelligence
generated_baseline_ref: build/reader_edition/chapters/failure-modes-of-ungoverned-intelligence.qmd
live_source_ref: chapters/failure-modes-of-ungoverned-intelligence.qmd@2917337c0
This file is a reader-prose derivative only. Preserve claim meaning,
support-state boundaries, source boundaries, proof/test status,
implementation horizons, and release blockers.
-->

# Failure Modes of Ungoverned Intelligence

The last chapter made authority explicit. This chapter asks the next question:
what should the stack do when a boundary fails, almost fails, or fires exactly
as designed?

The answer is not a catalog of spectacular disaster stories. The book needs a
failure model because ordinary engineering failures arrive first. A stale
context packet gets admitted. A route bypasses its authority ceiling. A tool
call borrows a credential from the wrong principal. A verifier is judged by
the same signal it was meant to test. A residual is deleted because the answer
looks good. A benchmark becomes the target rather than the pressure surface.

Those events matter because they are legible. A governed stack should fail in
a way that leaves a receipt, owner, containment path, residual question, and
learning obligation. Hidden success is more dangerous than visible failure
when nobody can audit what the success cost.

The reader promise of this chapter is diagnostic clarity. A human reader
should leave knowing how the ASI Stack turns risk language into an obligation
map: each failure mode names the boundary that should detect it, contain it,
preserve evidence about it, or block promotion until the residual is handled.

## Problem

Governance, reliability, and self-improvement claims need a failure model
before they need confidence.

Authority boundaries are useful only when their failures can be named. The
system does not have to fail first as a catastrophe. It can fail quietly
through goal misbinding, context pollution, evaluator drift, authority creep,
tool overreach, residual hiding, benchmark capture, or self-improvement loops
that ratify their own evidence.

The point of the taxonomy is not pessimism. It is interface design. Every
named failure should point to a later layer: VCM for context and taint,
Spinoza for claim verification and downgrades, Talos for jobs and tools, SCF
for replacement and lifecycle discipline, evidence ledgers for negative
results, and governance rights for appeal, fork, exit, and audit.

If a later chapter cannot say which failure it prevents, detects,
residualizes, or makes recoverable, then the mechanism may be interesting, but
it has not yet justified its place in the stack.

## Why existing approaches are insufficient

High-level safety language can be too large to test directly. Terms like
"alignment failure" and "unsafe autonomy" may be true, but they can hide the
smaller event that broke the system.

The smaller event is where engineering can start. Was the goal bound to the
wrong contract? Was context admitted after revocation? Did a summary promote
itself into evidence? Did a route exceed its authority ceiling? Did a verifier
time out and get treated as success? Did a negative result disappear from the
ledger? Did an evaluator become dependent on the component it was supposed to
judge?

External safety literature gives the taxonomy an outside pressure point.
Concrete AI Safety names accident-risk families such as side effects, reward
hacking, scalable oversight, safe exploration, and distributional shift. Work
on power-seeking pressure helps explain why broad objectives can create
option-preserving behavior. The ASI Stack uses those sources as positioning
comparators, not as validation of this repository's failure map.

The stack therefore treats failure modes as first-class architectural objects.
A failure should map to an invariant, detector, evidence record, containment
path, owner, and promotion blocker. If it cannot be mapped, the conclusion is
not that the failure is impossible. The conclusion is that the architecture is
missing a boundary.

Near misses become useful under that rule. A denied authority escalation, a
rejected context packet, a failed verifier, a quarantined route, or a preserved
residual is not a shameful blemish on the system. It is evidence that a
boundary fired. The architecture should record those events before polished
success stories dominate the ledger.

## Core Claim

Ungoverned intelligence fails through stack-level breakdowns before dramatic
catastrophic scenarios are needed. The live book keeps this claim at
`argument` support.

The claim is architectural. SCF, VCM, Talos, Spinoza, Field of God, VIEA, and
Simulation Scaling provide source-mapped vocabulary for lifecycle failure,
context failure, execution failure, verification failure, governance harm,
artifact/residual loss, and resource-contract failure. `vcm_editable` and
`moecot` remain connector-only/source-note mapped. These sources do not prove
that the stack detects or prevents the failures.

The current repository has a `failure_boundary_map` fixture and finite Lean
predicates for modeled invariant failure and unbounded-authority detection.
Those are useful surfaces, but they are not scenario coverage, deployed
failure detection, evaluator-independence proof, context-pollution containment,
or empirical incident evidence.

## Mechanism

Failure is treated as a boundary event, not an impression.

The mechanism is a Failure Boundary Map. It connects each risk to the boundary
that must detect it, contain it, preserve evidence about it, or keep it from
promoting a claim. SCF identifies replacement, evaluator, migration,
dependency, and recovery failures. VCM identifies context admission, taint,
revocation, adequacy, and hostile-text failures. Talos identifies job, tool,
approval, secret, audit, and replay failures. Spinoza identifies unsupported
claims, failed verification, contradiction, and protected-axiom failures.
VIEA adds artifact and residual loss. Simulation Scaling adds unbounded
simulation claims. MoECOT adds runtime overpromotion. Field of God preserves
the governance concern that power can erase agency, dignity, plurality, and
contestability if it is not translated into operational boundaries.

```{mermaid}
flowchart TB
  A["Human intent"] --> B["Goal binding failure"]
  B --> C["Authority creep"]
  C --> D["Context pollution"]
  D --> E["Evaluator drift"]
  E --> F["Tool / action overreach"]
  F --> G["Residual hiding"]
  G --> H["Unsupported promotion"]

  B -. "intent contracts" .-> I["Boundary"]
  C -. "authority transition" .-> I
  D -. "VCM admission / adequacy" .-> I
  E -. "independent evaluator" .-> I
  F -. "typed job + permission check" .-> I
  G -. "residual escrow" .-> I
  H -. "claim support state" .-> I
```

Read the map as a routing diagram for responsibility. The failure chain is not
there to dramatize danger. It is there to say where a broken contract should
go. Each risk is connected to the boundary that should detect it, contain it,
or preserve the residual.

That is why the taxonomy appears near the front of the book. Later chapters
are not allowed to present impressive machinery in isolation. They have to
answer a failure obligation. Which failure does this layer prevent? Which one
does it detect? Which one does it residualize? Which one does it make
recoverable?

The failure map should be boring enough to test. Every row needs a trigger,
protected invariant, detection route, containment action, evidence record,
downstream owner, promotion blocker, normalization guard, learning path, and
non-claim. Severity, reversibility, recurrence, and escape path matter too: a
minor reversible failure can become serious if it recurs without learning, and
a high-severity failure can remain contained if the escape path is blocked and
the residual is preserved.

## Interfaces

Failure analysis enters the stack through a Failure Boundary Map.

Governance limits authority creep. VCM limits context pollution. Verification
limits false certainty. Execution limits side effects. Evidence ledgers keep
negative results, denials, residuals, and near misses from disappearing.

At minimum, the record should say:

- what failed or nearly failed;
- which layer and contract were affected;
- which protected invariant was at risk;
- how severe and reversible the event was;
- whether the event is recurring;
- which detector or boundary saw it;
- what containment action happened;
- which receipt or evidence record was produced;
- who owns the downstream repair;
- which promotion is blocked;
- what escape path remains;
- what guard prevents normalization;
- what learning path follows;
- which source mappings explain the taxonomy;
- what support-state effect, if any, is allowed;
- which non-claims must travel with the record.

This interface lets later chapters prove that their machinery is not
decorative. A context chapter should point back to context-pollution failures.
An execution chapter should point back to tool and side-effect failures. A
benchmark chapter should point back to evaluator drift and residual hiding. A
self-improvement chapter should point back to recovery failure and procedural
self-ratification.

Failure receipts cover both blocked and realized failures. A blocked failure
receipt is not a victory lap and not a support-state promotion. It is a trace
that the boundary was exercised and a pointer to what evidence would be needed
before the boundary could be trusted more broadly.

## Invariants and Failure Modes

The first invariant is coverage: every serious risk needs an owning boundary.
The second is independence: an evaluator, verifier, or governance process
should not be silently modified by the component it is judging. The third is
memory: failed attempts, negative results, denials, and residuals stay visible
because future improvement depends on them.

Silent normalization is the enemy. A repeated failure should not become
ordinary behavior because operators stop recording it or because the system
learns to route around the detector. Failure ownership cannot transfer quietly
either. The receiving layer must accept the residual before the risk can move.

The obvious failures are evaluator capture, memory poisoning, tool/action
overreach, and compression that hides residual complexity. The more subtle
failure is failure laundering: a run fails a boundary, then the narrative
shifts to a narrower success metric while the blocked condition, denial, or
residual disappears from the evidence path.

The taxonomy can also fail. It can become too broad to test, too narrow to
catch novel risks, too stale to reflect the current architecture, or too
ceremonial to affect routing and promotion. That is why the next chapter turns
directly to evidence states. A failure mode matters only if it can block
promotion, force review, create residual escrow, or change the design.

## Minimum Viable Implementation

The smallest useful artifact is a `failure_boundary_map` schema plus a small
set of examples for authority creep, context pollution, evaluator drift, tool
overreach, and residual hiding.

Those examples should not claim the risks are solved. They should establish
that the book can represent the failure class, affected contracts, event
state, severity, reversibility, recurrence, protected invariant, receipt,
promotion blocker, containment path, normalization guard, learning path,
source refs, support-state effect, and boundary owner.

The failure taxonomy detector and mitigation-boundary probe adds a bounded
synthetic version of that harness. It checks two synthetic failure incidents:
one authority-creep attempt blocked at an authority ceiling, and one
Goodhart/evaluator-drift incident residualized through evaluator freeze and
review escalation. It also rejects seven bad paths: missing failure classes,
missing boundary owners, mitigation without receipts, authority creep treated
as ordinary operation, Goodhart/proxy failure with no residual, recurrence
without escalation, and support-state promotion from the fixture. This is not a deployed failure detector.

The current repository now has that synthetic diagnostic start: a
schema/example fixture, finite Lean predicates over modeled failure records,
and the bounded detector probe. Runtime detection, prevention, mitigation
effectiveness, evaluator independence, context containment, and claim-verifier
quality remain open.

## Beyond the State of the Art

The mature failure layer is a failure radar for ordinary stack breakdowns.

Instead of waiting for abstract risk language or dramatic failure, the stack
would classify breakdowns by layer, map each one to an invariant, attach a
falsification test, preserve the evidence record, and route the residual to
the layer that owns repair. Governance would limit authority creep. VCM would
limit context pollution. Verification would limit false certainty. Execution
would limit side effects. Evidence ledgers would keep negative results from
being smoothed away.

A risk that cannot be mapped to a boundary would remain a residual rather than
becoming vague fear or rhetorical urgency. Evaluator capture, memory
poisoning, tool overreach, and hidden residual complexity would trigger
quarantine, rollback, escalation, SCF review, or a claim-state block before
they normalize as ordinary behavior.

This remains an architectural target. The support state stays at `argument`
until failure records are mapped to invariant checks, falsification tests,
evaluator-capture probes, negative-result retention, and residual/escalation
routing that preserve failures instead of smoothing them away.

## Summary

Failure Modes of Ungoverned Intelligence gives the stack its obligation map.
Each failure names a boundary that must detect, contain, and preserve
evidence: authority for overreach, VCM for polluted context, verification for
false certainty, execution controls for side effects, and evidence ledgers for
residuals and negative results.

The chapter is not saying that the stack already prevents these failures. It
is saying that future stack layers have to answer them. A failure mode should
have somewhere to go. If it cannot produce a receipt, owner, containment
action, residual, or review path, the architecture has named a worry but has
not yet built a boundary.

Visible failure is the beginning of governance. Hidden success is what the
stack has to distrust.

## Handoff

Failure records are useful only when the book can say what kind of evidence
would repair, preserve, or downgrade them. Evidence States and Claim
Discipline therefore follows the failure taxonomy with the support-state
machinery that keeps prevention, detection, tests, proofs, source notes, and
negative results from collapsing into confidence. Risk language becomes
governed claim language: each assertion about failure has to carry the support
it has earned.
